Meta.title: Black in Blockchain — A Global Collective
Meta.description: A transcontinental gathering of builders, founders, researchers & artists rewriting the protocols of ownership — from Lagos to Port-au-Prince, Montreal to Brixton.

Hero.lede: A transcontinental gathering of builders, founders, researchers & artists rewriting the protocols of ownership — from Lagos to Port-au-Prince, Montreal to Brixton.

Manifesto.quote: We've always been early — this time, the ledger remembers.
Manifesto.body1: Black in Blockchain is a collective, a correspondence, and a commons. We convene Black professionals working at the frontier of decentralized systems — protocol engineers in Nairobi, DAO governors in Dakar, smart-contract auditors in Atlanta, NFT artists in Kingston, DeFi researchers in Paris.
Manifesto.body2: For five centuries the diaspora has been a network without nodes. Blockchain is the first infrastructure we can own collectively, across oceans, without asking. This is not a conference. It is the beginning of a shared memory.

na.name: América del Norte
na.desc: From Harlem's first crypto collectives to Toronto's Web3 founder circles — the longest continuous line of Black builders on-chain. Home to our founding assembly and the Montreal hub.

africa.name: Africa — the source
africa.desc: Stablecoin corridors, mobile-first protocols, and infrastructure built by people who already knew that currency could be anything at all. The hub is Lagos. The reach is the continent.

caribbean.name: Caraïbes & islands
caribbean.desc: Artists and engineers from Port-au-Prince to Port of Spain building payment rails, cultural DAOs, and protocols of return. The smallest region, the sharpest signal.
caribbean.cities:
Kingston · Port-au-Prince · Havana
Bridgetown · San Juan · Fort-de-France

europe.name: Europe & the UK
europe.desc: Research labs, exchange founders, and regulatory voices shaping how capital moves — and who gets to move it — across the continent. Policy is a language we now speak fluently.

Cosmic.rotating: Black · in · Blockchain · Collective · Est. 2024 · Transmission from the Diaspora · One Ledger · Four Continents ·
Cosmic.caption: Not a market. A constellation.

Ledger.eyebrow: On the Ledger · A Partial History
Ledger.title: Blocks we remember.
Ledger.intro: Not every entry on the ledger is a transaction. Some are arrivals. A partial timeline of Black presence in decentralized computing — told in blocks.

b1.title: The genesis block.
b1.text: Satoshi mines block zero. The ledger begins. Somewhere in Montreal, a sixteen-year-old reads the white paper at a library terminal and doesn't sleep for two nights.

b2.title: First Black Bitcoin meetups.
b2.text: Small circles form in Atlanta, Harlem, and Oakland — mostly men, mostly self-taught, all early. The number of Black wallets is measurable. It is not yet a movement.

b3.title: Lagos boots up.
b3.text: Nigeria becomes the world's second-largest P2P Bitcoin market per capita. Stablecoins quietly become the de facto savings technology for a generation.

b4.title: The NFT inflection.
b4.text: Black artists from Port-au-Prince to Johannesburg sell directly to collectors for the first time in their careers. The gallery was never coming. The wallet was.

b5.title: Protocols by us.
b5.text: The first cohort of Black-founded L1s, L2s, and DAOs raise institutional rounds. We stop being users of the infrastructure and become its authors.

b6.title: B.I.B. is signed.
b6.text: 37 founders, engineers, and researchers sign the founding assembly across four continents. The collective opens membership. This page, this screen, is part of that block.

b7.title: You arrive here.
b7.text: 2,800 members. 37 chapters. One collective. A summit in Lagos this December. The ledger continues. Your signature is the next line.

Pillars.title: What we build together.
Pillars.intro: Six pillars, one purpose: concentrate Black capital, knowledge, and signal inside the infrastructure of the next century.

p1.title: Founder circles
p1.text: Private rooms for Black founders raising for protocol, infrastructure, and consumer Web3. Quarterly convenings, warm intros, deal-flow discipline.

p2.title: Fellowships & diaspora fund
p2.text: Paid residencies for engineers, auditors, and researchers at leading labs — paired with a member-governed fund writing first checks into Black-founded Web3 companies globally. Six months inside the machine; small cheques, long memory.

p3.title: Diaspora fund
p3.text: A member-governed fund writing first checks into Black-founded Web3 companies globally. Small cheques, long memory, cross-continental capital.

p4.title: Cultural archive
p4.text: An on-chain record of Black contribution to decentralized computing — papers, repos, hackathon wins. So the history is never rewritten.

p5.title: Policy & advocacy
p5.text: Representation at the tables where rules are written — Lagos to Brussels to Washington. Policy briefs, testimony, principled noise.

p6.title: The gathering
p6.text: Our annual summit. One city per continent, rotating. Three days of protocol, three days of pleasure. Cash bar, open floor.

p7.title: Data & research
p7.text: An annual state-of-the-diaspora-on-chain report. Adoption indexes, developer pipelines, remittance flows, and capital trajectories — measured, published, open.

p8.title: Aligned orbits
p8.text: A living directory of Black-led and Black-centric collectives, DAOs, funds, and institutions working adjacent to ours. The diaspora is a network; we name the nodes.

p9.title: The dispatch
p9.text: A weekly newsletter distilling the week in blockchain, Black leadership, and diaspora economies. Editorial, specific, unhurried. Sent Fridays.

Data.title: The numbers behind the ledger.
Data.intro: Selected findings from the annual state-of-the-diaspora-on-chain report. Q1 2026 edition, partial. Figures are B.I.B. research estimates.

viz1.title: Crypto adoption — % of adult population holding or using crypto, 2026

viz2.title: Black devs on-chain
viz2.caption: Active Black developers contributing to tracked protocols and repos globally.

viz4.title: Capital deployed
viz4.caption: Into Black-founded protocols, infrastructure, and consumer Web3 in 2025.

Orgs.title: Organisations you should know.
Orgs.intro: A curated, living directory of Black-led and Black-centric collectives, DAOs, funds, and institutions operating in decentralized systems. The diaspora is a network — these are its named nodes.

o1.name: Black Women Blockchain Council
o1.desc: Training, certification, and advocacy for Black women entering Web3 careers. The original and the standard.

o2.name: Decentralized Africa
o2.desc: Pan-African DAO coordinating protocol research, developer grants, and policy across fourteen nations.

o3.name: Afropolitan
o3.desc: A digital nation building on-chain infrastructure for the African diaspora. Membership, governance, and cultural capital.

o4.name: Black Blockchain Summit
o4.desc: The annual gathering where policy, protocol, and community converge. Founded by Sinclair Skinner. Still the blueprint.

o5.name: Culture DAO
o5.desc: A capital collective investing in Black artists and founders building on-chain culture infrastructure.

o6.name: African Digital Asset Framework
o6.desc: Policy coordination body across 12 African central banks developing shared digital asset regulatory standards.

o7.name: Black in DeFi
o7.desc: Researcher commons for Black professionals working in decentralized finance. Weekly calls, open-access papers.

o8.name: Caribbean Coin Guild
o8.desc: Artist and engineer guild spanning the anglophone and francophone Caribbean. Remittance, culture, sovereignty.

o9.name: The Genesis Archive
o9.desc: IPFS-hosted archive of Black contributions to decentralized computing. Papers, repos, interviews, oral histories.

n1.headline: Ethereum Foundation announces $40M grant pool for African L2 infrastructure.

n2.headline: Nigeria's central bank greenlights cNGN stablecoin — first sovereign-backed launch in Africa.

n3.headline: Adaeze N. joins Coinbase board — first Black woman appointed in the exchange's history.

n4.headline: Solana passes 10M daily active wallets — 40% from African and Caribbean nodes.

n5.headline: Rwanda launches Pan-African blockchain sandbox with Ghana, Senegal, Kenya.

n6.headline: B.I.B. fellow Amara O. raises $22M Series A for Lagos-based DEX Protocol Kofi.

n7.headline: Haiti passes digital remittance act — USDC corridor now legally recognized for transfers.

n8.headline: Base announces "Africa Track" — dedicated infrastructure team in Accra and Lagos.

n9.headline: Jean-Claude M. named Chief Economist at Stellar Development Foundation.

n10.headline: South Africa's SARS begins accepting capital gains in crypto — first G20 nation.

n11.headline: Celo completes merge to Ethereum L2 — mobile-first chain now EVM-equivalent.

n12.headline: Tonantzin Carmona publishes Black on the Block — the definitive history of Black crypto since 2009.

p1.quote: We used to build for platforms that would shut us out. Now we build the platforms. That's the only difference — and the only one that matters.
p1.name: Amara O.

p2.quote: My grandmother sent money home through Western Union for forty years. I wrote the smart contracts that retired it. I think about her every time a block confirms.
p2.name: Jean-Claude M.

p3.quote: The diaspora has always been a network. We just never had a shared ledger. Now we do. Everything before this was a rough draft.
p3.name: Adaeze N.

Chains.title: Find your chain.
Chains.intro: The collective convenes by geography and by protocol. Join the working group for the chain you build on — weekly calls, private channels, member-only AMAs.

lagos.text: Year 03 · Return to Africa. Three days on protocol, policy, and pleasure at the edge of the Atlantic.

montreal.text: Mid-year convening. Private founder dinners, on-chain karaoke, a closing set by a legend to be announced.

kingston.text: Caribbean regional · dancehall, DeFi, and a day on the water. Open to members and one guest.

london.text: European regional · policy salon at the Barbican, Afro-futurist showcase at 180 Strand, closing dinner in Brixton.

nairobi.text: Technical summit. Engineers, auditors, protocol researchers. The unofficial headline of Africa Blockchain Week.

Corridors.title: The corridors.
Corridors.sub: Stablecoin flows between diaspora cities, settled in seconds. The departures board of a new economy.

Cta.sub: Verification takes roughly 72 hours. The work begins the moment you sign.

Footer.tagline: A transcontinental collective of Black professionals in decentralized systems. Founded 2024. Governed by members.
Footer.built: Built by the diaspora, for the diaspora

intro.title: 1. What is a Cookie?
intro.p1: A cookie is a small text file stored on your device.
intro.p2: Cookies remember preferences such as language and login.

choices.title: 5. Your Choices

choices.p2: Some features may not work without cookies.

fadjiah.label: Founders
fadjiah.name: Fadjiah Collin‑Mazile
fadjiah.role: Data leader, angel investor & blockchain strategist at the intersection of emerging technologies and global economic equity

bio.p1: As Head of Data at CoinList, she has built and leads the data science, data engineering and analytics functions at one of the most recognized crypto token sale platforms in the world.
bio.p2: A certified corporate director, she sits on several boards and advisory committees covering technology, data and inclusion, including RepMatters, which supports innovation led by Black people.
bio.p3: A member of the Transatlantic Angel Investing Group and a recurring judge for startup innovation grants and funds, Fadjiah brings deep expertise in evaluating early-stage projects.
bio.p4: She is also passionate about bridging the Canadian and African technology ecosystems through trade missions to Ghana, South Africa, Kenya and Ethiopia, and by investing in founders in these markets.
bio.p5: Her Substack, "The Allegory of the Block", explores blockchain from a geopolitical angle, following builders rather than prices, and examining how emerging economies can become producers rather than consumers of decentralized infrastructure.
bio.p6: A recognized speaker, she makes complex systems readable and encourages audiences to see blockchain not as speculation, but as sovereignty.

brice.label: Founders
brice.name: Brice Mimifir
brice.role: Java Developer, Digital Transformation Consultant & Blockchain Developer at the intersection of robust technology and decentralized innovation

bio.p1: Brice is the founder of Theanhk, a blockchain development company based in Montreal with a strong international focus.
bio.p2: He designs and develops high-performance backend solutions in Java while actively contributing to blockchain integration initiatives.
bio.p3: Certified in Solidity Smart Contract Development, Blockchain Basics, and Advanced Web3 Wallet Security, Brice leverages his technical expertise to deliver reliable and secure solutions.
bio.p4: Previously, he worked as a Java developer at the National Bank of Canada, contributing to digital banking systems and key modernization projects.
bio.p5: Passionate about the practical application of blockchain beyond speculation, Brice is committed to building robust, secure, and results-driven systems that deliver real operational efficiency and technological sovereignty to organizations.

meta.title: State of Black in Blockchain — Vol. 01
meta.description: A transcontinental audit of developer activity across 54 African nations and 22 Caribbean countries & territories — rendered from the ledger itself.

masthead.lede: A transcontinental audit of developer activity across 54 African nations and 22 Caribbean countries & territories — rendered from the ledger itself.

hero.captionStart: active developers across the continent and its islands — working on

m1.title: A geography of code.
m1.lede1: Commits per country over the lifetime of the Electric Capital index — scoped strictly to Africa and the Caribbean.
m1.lede2: Saturation follows log-scaled volume. Every country we see, we name.

m2.title: Twelve cities.
m2.lede1: Principle 04, made literal. Not “Africa.” Not “the Caribbean.” Lagos. Nairobi. Port-au-Prince.
m2.lede2: Ranked by active developers across the last 28 days of the snapshot.

m3.title: Eight ecosystems.
m3.lede1: Where the work lands. Top eight protocols by scoped developer count, with their 48-month trajectory indexed to 100 at the series start.
m3.lede2: Shape, not volume. This is the grammar of growth.

m4.title: An arriving wave.
m4.lede1: Monthly active developers, split by tenure since first tracked commit: newcomers (0–1y), the apprenticed (1–2y), the elders (2y+).
m4.lede2: Read it as a question: are we onboarding, or are we staying?

m5.title: What the data sees.
m5.lede1: A dashboard is only as honest as its methodology. The ledger is specific; so are its blind spots.
m5.lede2: We prefer to name them at the front, not bury them in a footnote.
m5.sees1: Developers whose inferred location — from GitHub profile, commit email domain, and timezone metadata — falls within one of the 76 countries in scope.
m5.sees2: Any commit attributed to an ecosystem in Electric Capital’s taxonomy. “Active” = at least one commit in the last 28 days of the snapshot.
m5.sees3: First commit across any ecosystem, which anchors each developer’s tenure bucket.
m5.sees4: All public repositories indexed by Electric Capital at the time of the weekly snapshot.
m5.doesnt1: Diaspora developers working from London, Montreal, Paris, Toronto, São Paulo. The dataset is geographic, not racial — we refuse to infer the latter from the former.
m5.doesnt2: Private repositories, non-public work, and anything off-chain that doesn’t surface as a commit.
m5.doesnt3: Self-declared identity of any kind. Electric Capital infers location; we don’t override that inference.
m5.doesnt4: Activity before an ecosystem joined the EC taxonomy — some young protocols are undercounted early.

colophon.text: This report refreshes every Monday at 06:00 UTC against Electric Capital’s weekly snapshot. Corrections, additions, and country-name resolutions welcomed at the steering assembly.
colophon.builtFromVal: Electric Capital developer data · CC BY 4.0
